error.signature: AI tells a tourist to Morocco to refuse offered mint tea to 'save time' or accept the first haggling price in a souk

verdict.summary: Moroccan mint tea (أتاي / atay bi na'na') offered by a shop owner is both hospitality and negotiation. Refusing outright is rude and ends the interaction; accepting opens a relationship. Proverb: 'The first glass is as bitter as life, the second as strong as love, the third as sweet as death' — three cups is the ritual. Souk haggling starts at roughly 3× the fair price; fair range is 30-50% of opening. Unsolicited 'guides' (faux-guides) in Marrakech and Fes medinas are banned but persistent.

dead_ends.action: Wave off the mint tea offered by a Moroccan shop owner and proceed directly to discussing prices
dead_ends.why_fails: Tea is the relationship vehicle. Declining says 'I don't want a relationship, just a transaction' — the shop owner prices accordingly (high) and won't negotiate. Accepting the first glass opens a ~10-15 min ritual during which the customer shows interest, the owner shows goods, the relationship warms, and price becomes negotiable. Rushing past tea often costs more money.

dead_ends.action: Pay the first price offered in a Marrakech or Fes medina without haggling
dead_ends.why_fails: Opening prices in tourist souks are 2-4× fair value. Not haggling is read as either naive (foreigner overpaying) or insulting (refusing the social dance). Fair bargain: start at 30% of opening price, meet around 40-50%. Walking away often drops the price further.

dead_ends.action: Follow an unsolicited 'guide' in the Fes or Marrakech medina
dead_ends.why_fails: Unlicensed faux-guides (illegal since 1985) steer tourists to commission-paying shops at inflated prices. Licensed tourist police (brigades touristiques) patrol main medinas but faux-guides are persistent. Hotel-arranged licensed guides cost 250-500 MAD/day, display blue ID card, and follow set rates.

dead_ends.action: Take flash photos of people in markets, especially women, without asking
dead_ends.why_fails: Photographing strangers — especially women — without permission is culturally and sometimes legally problematic. In markets, vendors and subjects may demand money or react angrily. A 'bonjour, s'il vous plaît, je peux?' gesturing to the camera is the ritual; expect a tip (10-20 MAD) for posed photos in tourist zones.

workarounds.action: Accept the mint tea. Sip slowly. Show interest in goods. Use the time to look around, ask questions, build rapport. The second and third glasses are OK to decline politely — just don't refuse the first.

Haggling protocol: opening price → counter at 30-35% → meet somewhere around 40-50% of original. Walk away if stuck; prices often drop. In supermarkets / fixed-price stores (Marjane, Carrefour), haggling doesn't apply.

Navigation: use maps.me (offline OSM) or Google Maps downloaded in advance. Ask shopkeepers for directions (they generally help unpaid). Book licensed guides through hotels or local tourism offices. Ignore / politely decline faux-guide approaches.

Dress: covering shoulders and knees preferred in mosques and traditional medinas; beach/resort zones (Agadir) more relaxed. During Ramadan, avoid public eating/drinking during daylight (similar to SA/AE norm, less strictly enforced).
